try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.title: Read One Unstaged Edit
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The community site heading changed in `index.html`. Inspect only that file’s unstaged diff.
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.hint: Inspect the working-tree patch before changing the staging area.

help.concept: git diff shows working-tree changes that have not been staged.
help.hint_1: Focus on the removed and added lines for the path named in the task.
help.hint_2: The patch should contain only the edit described in the scenario.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash set -eu rm -rf community-site mkdir -p community-site git -C community-site init -q -b main git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner" git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local" cd community-site printf %s '

Community Hub

' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline' cd community-site printf %s '

Neighborhood Hub

' > index.html cd ..

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.title: Compare a Style Change
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The site palette changed in `styles.css`. Inspect that file without including the unrelated README edit.
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.hint: Inspect the working-tree patch before changing the staging area.

help.concept: A path-limited diff narrows review to one file without altering any changes.
help.hint_1: Focus on the removed and added lines for the path named in the task.
help.hint_2: The patch should contain only the edit described in the scenario.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'color: navy;
' > styles.css
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s '# Community Site
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'color: teal;
' > styles.css
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s '# Community Website
' > README.md
cd ..

' > styles.css
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s '# Community Site
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'color: teal;
' > styles.css
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s '# Community Website
' > README.md
cd ..

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.title: List the Files That Changed
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Two tracked files changed. Use the name-only diff view first, then inspect `app.js` in detail.
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.hint: Inspect the working-tree patch before changing the staging area.

help.concept: The name-only view reveals the scope of an edit before a detailed diff explains one file.
help.hint_1: Focus on the removed and added lines for the path named in the task.
help.hint_2: The patch should contain only the edit described in the scenario.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = false;
' > app.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const theme = "light";
' > config.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = true;
' > app.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const theme = "dark";
' > config.js
cd ..

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = false;
' > app.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const theme = "light";
' > config.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = true;
' > app.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const theme = "dark";
' > config.js
cd ..

sketch0.title: Status Names the File; Diff Shows the Edit
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
Short status tells you **which** tracked file changed. A plain diff tells you **how** its working copy differs from the staging area.

If `menu.txt` changes from `Tea` to `Tea and coffee`, the diff marks the old line with `-` and the new line with `+`. Those marks describe the comparison; they are not stored in the file.

Use the file-level map first, then open the line-level evidence for the path you intend to review.

To review one file without mixing in other edits, name the path explicitly:

```bash
git diff -- menu.txt
```

This shows only the unstaged patch for `menu.txt`. The exercise uses a different file, but the review decision is the same.

sketch1.title: Read a Patch Without Guessing
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
A patch contains file headers and one or more change blocks. In each block, unchanged context lines help you locate the edit, removed lines begin with `-`, and added lines begin with `+`.

Start with the body of the change. Ask: which sentence disappeared, which sentence replaced it, and does that match the task? The surrounding metadata becomes easier once the core edit is clear.

A diff is a review document, not merely command output.

A focused patch is easier to read than a repository-wide one:

```bash
git diff -- colors.css
```

Read the removed lines first, then the added lines, and use the unchanged context to locate the edit. The command leaves the file untouched.

sketch2.title: Separate Work Before It Becomes History
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
A repository may contain several valid edits that do not belong in the same commit. A navigation update and a corrected phone number can both be useful while still representing different purposes.

Reviewing unstaged diffs exposes those boundaries early. You can stage one file, leave the other for later, and write a message that accurately describes the resulting snapshot.

Focused history begins before staging, while the work is still easy to separate.

When several files changed, begin with a map and then open one patch:

```bash
git diff --name-only
git diff -- footer.txt
```

The first command lists every path with an unstaged tracked change. The second narrows the review to the file that belongs to the current purpose.

sketch3.title: Make Review the Default
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
A calm review loop uses short status for the map and diff for the detail. It catches debug text, unfinished wording, and unrelated edits before they enter the staging area.

When the patch matches the requested outcome, you can stage with confidence. When it does not, fix the file while the mistake is still only a working-tree draft.

The goal is not to run more commands. It is to make each later decision from evidence.

A final pre-staging review can pair detail with state:

```bash
git diff -- README.md
git status --short
```

The diff proves the wording is correct. Status confirms that the change remains in the working tree and has not been staged accidentally.
